Name: info_theoretic_model_compression
Title: Information-Theoretic Model Compression: A Rate-Distortion Approach
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that model compression can be formulated as a rate-distortion problem, where the goal is to minimize the number of bits required to represent a model while maintaining its predictive performance. We propose to use information-theoretic tools, such as entropy and mutual information, to quantify the complexity of the model and the distortion caused by compression.
Related Work: Recent works, such as EMQ, AMP-ViT, and SNIP, have focused on developing efficient methods for mixed-precision quantization and training. However, these approaches often rely on heuristic-based methods and lack a clear understanding of the fundamental limits of model compression. Our approach is distinct in that it provides a principled, information-theoretic framework for model compression, which can be used to derive achievable rates and distortion-rate curves.
Abstract: This paper proposes an information-theoretic approach to model compression, where the goal is to minimize the number of bits required to represent a model while maintaining its predictive performance. We formulate model compression as a rate-distortion problem and use tools from information theory, such as entropy and mutual information, to quantify the complexity of the model and the distortion caused by compression. We derive achievable rates and distortion-rate curves for various compression schemes and demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on several benchmark datasets.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments on several benchmark datasets, including ImageNet and CIFAR-10, to evaluate the performance of our information-theoretic approach to model compression. We will compare our approach to existing methods, such as EMQ and AMP-ViT, and evaluate the trade-off between compression rate and distortion. We will also investigate the effect of different compression schemes, such as quantization and pruning, on the distortion-rate curve.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk of our approach is that it may not be directly applicable to all types of models or datasets. Additionally, the computational complexity of our approach may be higher than that of existing methods, which could limit its scalability. However, we believe that the principled, information-theoretic framework provided by our approach will lead to a deeper understanding of the fundamental limits of model compression and inspire the development of more efficient and effective compression methods.
